content.text: Raw text extracted from PDFs, web pages, and DOCX files is never embedding-ready. It arrives with encoding artifacts, Unicode inconsistencies, collapsed whitespace, boilerplate text, and near-duplicate passages. Normalisation turns that noisy string into consistent, clean text before the chunker ever sees it. Skipping this step allows garbage to propagate into embeddings, degrading retrieval quality silently and permanently.

attrs.alt: Normalisation pipeline: Raw text → Unicode normalisation (NFC/NFKC) → encoding repair (mojibake) → whitespace normalisation → boilerplate and dedup removal → clean text string

content.text: Unicode defines multiple ways to represent the same visible character. The letter
can be stored as one precomposed codepoint (U+00E9) or as the letter
followed by a combining acute accent (U+0065 U+0301). Both look identical but compare as unequal, causing tokenisation inconsistencies and silently corrupting similarity scores.
Default for most text; é stays as one codepoint
Useful for accent stripping (remove combining chars after)
Combines NFKC benefits with split form; less common
is the best default. It composes combining characters (like NFC) and additionally folds compatibility characters—ligatures like
—into their base ASCII equivalents. This ensures embedding models see the same token regardless of the source format.

import unicodedata
import re

def normalise_unicode(text: str) -> str:
    # NFKC: fold ligatures, superscripts, fractions; compose combining chars
    text = unicodedata.normalize("NFKC", text)

    # Replace typographic quotes with straight ASCII equivalents
    text = text.replace("‘", "'").replace("’", "'")   # '' → ''
    text = text.replace("“", '"').replace("”", '"')   # "" → ""

    # Replace typographic dashes with hyphens / double-hyphens
    text = text.replace("—", " -- ").replace("–", "-")  # em/en dash

    # Replace ellipsis character with three dots
    text = text.replace("…", "...")

    return text

# Examples
print(repr(normalise_unicode("The ﬁrm’s revenue—up 12…")))
# 'The firm's revenue -- up 12...'

print(repr(normalise_unicode("café")))     # NFC é → unchanged
# 'café'
print(repr(normalise_unicode("café")))    # NFD e + combining → composed
# 'café'

, "character transformation") occurs when text encoded in one character set is decoded as a different one. The most common pattern in enterprise documents is Windows-1252 (or Latin-1) text that was mislabelled or processed as UTF-8:
Correctly decoded if source was UTF-8
(Fix Text For You) library detects and repairs mojibake automatically. It handles dozens of encoding mix-ups that regex cannot easily cover:

# pip install ftfy
import ftfy

# Mojibake examples
bad1 = "The firms Q3 results"
bad2 = "café revenue"
bad3 = "â€œprofitâ€ increased"    # UTF-8 bytes read as Latin-1

print(ftfy.fix_text(bad1))   # "The firm's Q3 results…"
print(ftfy.fix_text(bad2))   # "café revenue"
print(ftfy.fix_text(bad3))   # '"profit" increased'

# Fix an entire extracted text block
def repair_encoding(text: str) -> str:
    return ftfy.fix_text(
        text,
        fix_entities=True,    # decode HTML entities: & → &
        remove_control_chars=True,
        fix_latin_ligatures=True,
    )
ftfy's output is valid UTF-8; then NFKC folds ligatures. Running them in the wrong order can over-normalise repaired text.

Extracted text typically has three whitespace problems: extra spaces within lines (from PDF character positioning), excessive blank lines between paragraphs, and invisible Unicode whitespace characters that appear as spaces but are not.

import re

# Common invisible Unicode whitespace characters
INVISIBLE = (
    "\u00a0",   # non-breaking space
    "\u200b",   # zero-width space
    "\u200c",   # zero-width non-joiner
    "\u200d",   # zero-width joiner
    "\ufeff",   # BOM (byte order mark)
    "\u2060",   # word joiner
    "\u3000",   # ideographic space
)

def normalise_whitespace(text: str) -> str:
    # 1. Replace invisible whitespace with regular space
    for ch in INVISIBLE:
        text = text.replace(ch, " ")

    # 2. Collapse multiple spaces / tabs on the same line
    text = re.sub(r"[ \t]{2,}", " ", text)

    # 3. Strip trailing whitespace from each line
    lines = [line.rstrip() for line in text.splitlines()]

    # 4. Collapse runs of blank lines to at most two (paragraph break)
    cleaned = []
    blank_count = 0
    for line in lines:
        if line == "":
            blank_count += 1
            if blank_count <= 2:
                cleaned.append(line)
        else:
            blank_count = 0
            cleaned.append(line)

    return "\n".join(cleaned).strip()

# Test
messy = "Revenue   grew\n\n\n\nPage 3\n\n\n\nQ1 results"
print(repr(normalise_whitespace(messy)))
# 'Revenue grew\n\nPage 3\n\nQ1 results'

Boilerplate is text that repeats across pages and carries no information relevant to the query. Running headers, footers, page numbers, and legal disclaimers all pollute chunks and waste context window tokens in the generator.

import re
from collections import Counter

# Patterns that reliably indicate boilerplate
BOILERPLATE_PATTERNS = [
    re.compile(r"^\s*page\s+\d+\s*(of\s+\d+)?\s*$", re.I),      # "Page 3 of 12"
    re.compile(r"^\s*\d+\s*$"),                                        # bare page number
    re.compile(r"confidential|proprietary|all rights reserved", re.I),   # legal stubs
    re.compile(r"^\s*[\u2014\u2013-]{3,}\s*$"),                      # divider lines --- or ———
]

def is_boilerplate_line(line: str) -> bool:
    stripped = line.strip()
    if len(stripped) < 5:          # very short stubs
        return True
    for pat in BOILERPLATE_PATTERNS:
        if pat.search(stripped):
            return True
    return False

def remove_boilerplate_lines(text: str) -> str:
    lines = text.splitlines()
    clean = [l for l in lines if not is_boilerplate_line(l)]
    return "\n".join(clean)
Running headers and footers appear on every page with nearly identical text. You can detect them by counting how often each line appears across all pages:

Documents often contain repeated sections: a summary that duplicates the introduction, a disclaimer repeated in every section, or crawled web pages with overlapping content. Near-duplicates waste storage, inflate retrieval scores for repeated content, and push genuinely distinct information out of the top-K results.
MinHash with Locality-Sensitive Hashing (LSH) finds near-duplicate text in sublinear time—much faster than computing pairwise cosine similarity over large corpora:

# pip install datasketch
from datasketch import MinHash, MinHashLSH

def text_to_minhash(text: str, num_perm: int = 128) -> MinHash:
    m = MinHash(num_perm=num_perm)
    # Shingling: represent text as overlapping n-grams of words
    words = text.lower().split()
    for i in range(max(1, len(words) - 2)):
        shingle = " ".join(words[i:i+3])
        m.update(shingle.encode("utf8"))
    return m

def deduplicate_documents(
    docs: list[dict],   # list of {"text": ..., "metadata": ...}
    threshold: float = 0.85,
    num_perm: int = 128,
) -> list[dict]:
    """Remove near-duplicates; keep the first occurrence."""
    lsh = MinHashLSH(threshold=threshold, num_perm=num_perm)
    unique_docs = []

    for idx, doc in enumerate(docs):
        m = text_to_minhash(doc["text"], num_perm)
        key = f"doc_{idx}"

        result = lsh.query(m)    # find existing docs with Jaccard ≥ threshold
        if not result:
            lsh.insert(key, m)
            unique_docs.append(doc)
        # else: near-duplicate of an existing doc → skip

    return unique_docs

# Example
docs = [
    {"text": "Acme Corp reported revenue of $2.1B in Q3 2025.", "metadata": {"source": "press_release.pdf"}},
    {"text": "Acme Corp reported revenue of $2.1B in Q3 2025!", "metadata": {"source": "summary.pdf"}},  # near-dup
    {"text": "Q3 2025 gross margin improved to 62%.", "metadata": {"source": "press_release.pdf"}},
]
unique = deduplicate_documents(docs, threshold=0.85)
print(len(unique))   # 2  (second doc is a near-duplicate of the first)

Multi-lingual document corpora need language detection to route chunks to the correct embedding model or index shard. Mixing French and English documents in the same embedding space degrades cross-lingual retrieval unless you use a multilingual model.

# pip install langdetect  (or: pip install lingua-language-detector for higher accuracy)
from langdetect import detect, DetectorFactory

# Make detection deterministic
DetectorFactory.seed = 42

def detect_language(text: str, min_length: int = 50) -> str | None:
    """Return ISO 639-1 language code, or None if text is too short to detect reliably."""
    if len(text.split()) < min_length // 5:
        return None
    try:
        return detect(text[:2000])    # first 2000 chars is enough for detection
    except Exception:
        return None

# Examples
print(detect_language("The quarterly earnings report showed strong growth."))    # 'en'
print(detect_language("Le rapport financier montre une forte croissance."))       # 'fr'
print(detect_language("Die Umsatzzahlen zeigen ein starkes Wachstum."))          # 'de'
For higher accuracy, especially on short texts or uncommon languages, use
— it outperforms langdetect on short paragraphs and has lower false-positive rates.
All stages compose into a single
function called on every extracted text block before it reaches the text splitter:

import re
import unicodedata
import ftfy
from langdetect import detect, DetectorFactory

DetectorFactory.seed = 42

INVISIBLE_CHARS = "\u00a0\u200b\u200c\u200d\ufeff\u2060\u3000"
QUOTE_MAP = {
    "\u2018": "'", "\u2019": "'",
    "\u201c": '"', "\u201d": '"',
    "\u2026": "...",
    "\u2014": " -- ", "\u2013": "-",
}
BOILERPLATE = re.compile(
    r"^\s*(page\s+\d+|\d+\s*$|confidential|all rights reserved|"
    r"[\u2014\u2013-]{3,})\s*$", re.I
)

def normalise(text: str) -> str | None:
    """Full normalisation pipeline. Returns None if text is empty after cleaning."""
    # 1. Repair encoding (mojibake)
    text = ftfy.fix_text(text, fix_entities=True, remove_control_chars=True)

    # 2. NFKC Unicode normalisation
    text = unicodedata.normalize("NFKC", text)

    # 3. Typographic character substitution
    for src, dst in QUOTE_MAP.items():
        text = text.replace(src, dst)

    # 4. Remove invisible Unicode whitespace
    for ch in INVISIBLE_CHARS:
        text = text.replace(ch, " ")

    # 5. Per-line filtering and whitespace collapse
    lines = []
    blank_run = 0
    for line in text.splitlines():
        line = re.sub(r"[ \t]{2,}", " ", line).rstrip()
        if not line:
            blank_run += 1
            if blank_run <= 2:
                lines.append("")
        elif BOILERPLATE.match(line):
            pass   # drop boilerplate line
        else:
            blank_run = 0
            lines.append(line)

    text = "\n".join(lines).strip()
    if len(text) < 20:
        return None   # too short after cleaning — discard

    return text

# Integration with LangChain Document pipeline
from langchain_core.documents import Document

def normalise_documents(docs: list[Document]) -> list[Document]:
    clean = []
    for doc in docs:
        cleaned_text = normalise(doc.page_content)
        if cleaned_text:
            clean.append(Document(
                page_content=cleaned_text,
                metadata={
                    **doc.metadata,
                    "language": detect_language(cleaned_text),
                },
            ))
    return clean
ﬁ appears as a single codepoint; tokenised differently from fi
Always apply NFKC before embedding or chunking
don\x92t and don't tokenise as different words
Collapsing all whitespace to one space
Paragraph breaks disappear; heading/body runs together
Preserve \n\n paragraph separators; only collapse spaces/tabs within lines
Numeric data like table cells ("$ 2 . 1 B") merged
Use length threshold > 4 chars, not all short lines; inspect output
Repeated summary sections over-weight retrieval scores
Apply MinHash LSH dedup at 0.85 Jaccard before chunking
Detect fenced code blocks and skip normalisation inside them
NFKC normalisation folds Unicode ligatures (ﬁ→fi), superscripts, and combining characters — run it on all extracted text.
ftfy repairs mojibake (encoding mix-ups between Windows-1252 and UTF-8) that Unicode normalisation cannot fix.
Normalise whitespace per-line: collapse spaces/tabs within a line but preserve paragraph-level newlines.
Frequency-based header/footer detection removes running page elements without format-specific rules.
MinHash LSH deduplication removes near-duplicate passages in sublinear time — threshold 0.85 Jaccard is a safe default.
Detect language per document and store it in metadata to route chunks to the correct index or embedding model.
Never normalise inside code blocks — indentation is semantic.
